3.6. Observing cells under a microscope>3.6. Observing cells under a microscope
3.7. The cell and its environment>7.1 Passive transport

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A scientist observes a sample under a microscope and sees many small, identical structures that contain DNA and can reproduce independently. Which principle of cell theory does this observation most directly support?

options.A: Cells are the smallest unit of life.
options.B: All living organisms are composed of one or more cells.
options.C: Cells arise only from pre-existing cells.

questions.hint: Consider that the observation is about the structures themselves being the smallest independent units.
questions.explanation: Observing that the smallest structures seen contain DNA and reproduce independently supports that cells are the smallest unit capable of life processes. Option B describes organisms, C describes origin, and D is false for prokaryotes.

source_trace.content_anchor: Cells are the smallest unit of life.

explanations_tiered.definition: Cell theory includes the principle that cells are the smallest structural and functional unit of life.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This principle means that any structure smaller than a cell cannot carry out all life processes independently. Viruses, for example, are not considered living because they lack cellular machinery.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A cell is like the smallest Lego brick that can still be a building block; you can't go smaller and still have a working block.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The observation that the smallest independent, reproducing structures are cells supports the principle that the cell is the fundamental unit of life. Nothing smaller can survive or reproduce on its own.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This principle underlies the definition of life: a cell is the minimal autonomous system capable of metabolism, homeostasis, and reproduction. Subcellular components like ribosomes cannot function independently.
reasoning_steps: Identify what the scientist observed: independent, reproducing structures.
Recall that cells are the smallest units that can do this.
Match to the principle about cells being the smallest unit of life.
misconceptions: Some think viruses are cells, but they are not; they cannot reproduce without a host.
Organelles like mitochondria have their own DNA but cannot live independently.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Cell theory: cell is smallest unit of life.

glossary_entry.definition: The principle that the cell is the most basic structure that can perform all life functions.

flashcard.front: Why is the cell considered the smallest unit of life?
flashcard.back: Nothing smaller than a cell can independently carry out all life processes like metabolism and reproduction.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What evidence shows the cell is the smallest unit of life?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Nothing smaller than a cell can independently carry out all life processes like reproduction.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The principle that the cell is the most basic structure capable of independent life functions.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use this to discuss why viruses are not considered living—they are not cells and cannot reproduce without a host.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. The observation supports that cells are the smallest unit of life. B describes organisms composed of cells, C is about origin, D is false for prokaryotes.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following features is characteristic of a prokaryotic cell?

options.A: Contains a nucleus with linear chromosomes
options.B: Has membrane-bound organelles such as mitochondria
options.C: Contains a single circular DNA molecule in the nucleoid region

questions.hint: Recall the location and form of DNA in bacteria.
questions.explanation: Prokaryotic cells have a single circular DNA molecule located in the nucleoid region, not enclosed by a membrane. They lack a nucleus and membrane-bound organelles. Options A, B, and D describe eukaryotic cells.

source_trace.content_anchor: Prokaryotic cells such as bacterial cells don't have membrane-bound organelles or a nucleus. DNA present, not associated with proteins, circular plasmids may also be present.

explanations_tiered.definition: Prokaryotic cells lack a nucleus and have a single circular DNA molecule in the nucleoid region.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Bacteria and archaea are prokaryotes. Their DNA is not enclosed in a membrane and is circular, unlike linear eukaryotic chromosomes. They also lack other membrane-bound organelles.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A prokaryotic cell is like a studio apartment: everything is in one open room, and the DNA is just a single loop floating around.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Prokaryotic DNA is a single circular chromosome located in the nucleoid region, not surrounded by a nuclear envelope. They also lack membrane-bound organelles like the ER or mitochondria.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The prokaryotic chromosome is haploid and circular. Plasmids, small circular DNA molecules, are also common. DNA replication and transcription occur in the nucleoid simultaneously with translation due to the absence of a nucleus.
reasoning_steps: Recall that prokaryotes have no nucleus.
Recall that their DNA is circular and in the nucleoid.
Select the option describing that.
misconceptions: Some think prokaryotes have a nucleus (pro = before, karyon = nucleus, so 'before nucleus').
Prokaryotes are sometimes thought to have no DNA; they have DNA but not in a nucleus.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Prokaryote: circular DNA in nucleoid, no nucleus.

glossary_entry.definition: The region in a prokaryotic cell where the circular DNA is located, not enclosed by a membrane.

flashcard.front: Where is DNA found in a prokaryotic cell?
flashcard.back: DNA is in the nucleoid region as a single circular molecule.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What type of DNA do prokaryotes have?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Prokaryotes have a single circular DNA molecule in the nucleoid region.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A cell without a nucleus; DNA is circular in the nucleoid.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Differentiate between eukaryotic and prokaryotic cells
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Prokaryotic DNA is a single circular molecule in the nucleoid. A, B, and D describe eukaryotic cells.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following structures would you expect to find in a eukaryotic cell but NOT in a prokaryotic cell?
questions.hint: Think about which organelle has a double membrane and its own DNA.
questions.explanation: Mitochondria are membrane-bound organelles found only in eukaryotes. Both cell types have ribosomes, cell membranes, and DNA, but prokaryotes lack membrane-bound organelles like mitochondria.

source_trace.content_anchor: Eukaryotes such as plants and animals cells have membrane-bound organelles and a nucleus.

explanations_tiered.definition: Eukaryotic cells contain membrane-bound organelles such as mitochondria, which are absent in prokaryotes.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Membrane-bound organelles are the key distinction. Both cell types share ribosomes (though different sizes), cell membranes, and genetic material.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Mitochondria are like special rooms in a house; prokaryotic cells are like one-room studios that don't have those rooms.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Both prokaryotes and eukaryotes have ribosomes, cell membranes, and DNA. However, only eukaryotes have membrane-bound organelles like mitochondria, the nucleus, and the ER.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The endosymbiotic theory proposes that mitochondria originated from prokaryotes engulfed by ancestral eukaryotes. This explains why mitochondria have double membranes and their own circular DNA, and why they are absent in prokaryotes.
reasoning_steps: Recall which structures are membrane-bound organelles.
Recall that prokaryotes lack membrane-bound organelles.
Identify the option that is a membrane-bound organelle.
misconceptions: Some think ribosomes are not in prokaryotes; both have ribosomes but they differ in size (70S vs 80S).
All eukaryotes have mitochondria, but some anaerobic eukaryotes have reduced mitochondria called mitosomes.

glossary_entry.definition: A subcellular structure enclosed by a membrane, found only in eukaryotic cells.

flashcard.front: What do eukaryotes have that prokaryotes lack?
flashcard.back: Membrane-bound organelles like mitochondria and a nucleus.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Mitochondria are membrane-bound and only in eukaryotes. Both cell types have ribosomes (A), cell membranes (B), and DNA (D).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A cell is placed in a solution and the cell membrane allows oxygen to enter but prevents a large protein from leaving. What property of the cell membrane does this demonstrate?
questions.hint: The membrane allows some substances through but not others.
questions.explanation: Selective permeability means the cell membrane allows certain molecules (like oxygen) to pass while blocking others (like large proteins). It is not rigid (A), not only active (C), and not universally permeable (D).

source_trace.content_anchor: Cell membrane is a very thin selectively permeable structure.

explanations_tiered.definition: Selective permeability is the property of a membrane that allows some substances to pass through but not others.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The cell membrane's phospholipid bilayer blocks large polar molecules and ions while allowing small nonpolar molecules like oxygen to diffuse through. Proteins regulate passage of other substances.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The cell membrane is like a bouncer at a club: it lets some things in (oxygen) and keeps others out (large proteins).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Selective permeability is essential for homeostasis. Small nonpolar molecules like oxygen and carbon dioxide diffuse freely, while large proteins and charged ions require channels or carriers.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The hydrophobic core of the lipid bilayer is a barrier to polar molecules. Membrane proteins provide selective channels, carriers, and pumps. This selective permeability creates concentration gradients used for cellular work.
reasoning_steps: Observe that oxygen entered but the protein did not.
Recall that this differential passage is called selective permeability.
Eliminate options about rigidity, active-only transport, and universal permeability.
misconceptions: Some think the cell membrane is a solid barrier; it is fluid and selectively permeable.
Selective permeability is sometimes confused with active transport; active transport is a specific mechanism that requires energy.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Selective permeability: some things pass, some don't.

glossary_entry.definition: The property of a membrane that allows certain molecules to pass through while blocking others.

flashcard.back: The ability of the cell membrane to allow some substances to pass while blocking others.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The membrane allowing oxygen but blocking protein demonstrates selective permeability. Rigidity (A) is not the main property shown, active transport (C) requires energy, universal permeability (D) would allow both through.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In preparing a cheek cell slide, a student uses methylene blue. Why is this stain used?

options.A: To kill bacteria that might be on the cheek
options.B: To make the cell wall more visible
options.C: To stain the nucleus and increase contrast
options.D: To separate cells from each other

questions.hint: Methylene blue is a basic dye that binds to negatively charged molecules in the nucleus.
questions.explanation: Methylene blue stains the nucleus (and other structures) by binding to DNA and RNA, increasing contrast so cells can be seen clearly under the microscope. Cheek cells lack cell walls (B), and it does not primarily kill bacteria or separate cells (A, D).

source_trace.content_anchor: Add two to three drops of methylene blue dye. Which cellular structure is more visible following staining?

explanations_tiered.definition: Methylene blue is a basic stain that binds to negatively charged cellular components like DNA and RNA.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Staining with methylene blue makes the nucleus and other organelles visible by adding color contrast against the transparent cytoplasm. It is commonly used for animal cells like cheek epithelium.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Methylene blue is like a marker that colors the nucleus so you can see it, like drawing with a colored pen on clear plastic.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Methylene blue binds to nucleic acids in the nucleus, staining it dark blue. This provides contrast, making the nucleus and sometimes other organelles visible under brightfield microscopy.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Methylene blue is a cationic dye (positively charged). It binds to anionic sites in the cell, particularly DNA and RNA in the nucleus and rough ER. This metachromatic dye can also stain mast cell granules.
reasoning_steps: Recall the purpose of staining in microscopy.
Recall which cell structure methylene blue highlights.
Select the option about staining the nucleus for contrast.
misconceptions: Some think methylene blue is only for plant cells; it is commonly used for animal cells.
Methylene blue is sometimes thought to preserve cells; it increases contrast but does not preserve.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Methylene blue: stains nucleus for visibility.

glossary_entry.definition: A basic dye used to stain cell nuclei for microscopic observation.

flashcard.front: What does methylene blue stain in cheek cells?
flashcard.back: Methylene blue stains the nucleus, making it visible under the microscope.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Methylene blue stains the nucleus to increase contrast. It does not primarily kill bacteria (A), cheek cells lack cell walls (B), and it does not separate cells (D).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.structural_anchor: Factors influencing the rate of diffusion
questions.question: How does increasing temperature affect the rate of diffusion across a cell membrane?

options.A: It decreases the rate because molecules move more slowly
options.B: It increases the rate because molecules have more kinetic energy
options.C: It has no effect because diffusion is not temperature-dependent
options.D: It stops diffusion completely because membranes become impermeable

questions.hint: Think about how heat affects particle motion.
questions.explanation: Higher temperature increases the kinetic energy of molecules, causing them to move faster and diffuse more rapidly. Options A, C, and D are incorrect because diffusion rate increases with temperature, not decreases, and it never stops due to temperature alone.

source_trace.structural_element: Factors influencing the rate of diffusion
source_trace.content_anchor: Temperature: increase in temperature causes an increase in the kinetic energy that molecules and ions possess enabling them to move faster.

explanations_tiered.definition: Increasing temperature increases molecular kinetic energy, which speeds up diffusion.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Temperature is directly proportional to average kinetic energy. Faster-moving molecules collide more frequently and spread more quickly down their concentration gradient.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Heat makes molecules bounce around faster, like kids in a playground running faster when it's warm, so they spread out quicker.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Higher temperature increases the speed of random molecular motion. This leads to more frequent collisions and a faster net movement of molecules from high to low concentration.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The diffusion coefficient \(D\) increases with temperature according to the Arrhenius equation: \(D = D_0 e^{-E_a/(RT)}\). A 10°C rise typically doubles the diffusion rate in biological systems.
reasoning_steps: Recall that temperature increases kinetic energy.
Recall that faster molecules diffuse more quickly.
Select the option linking higher temperature to faster diffusion.
misconceptions: Some think cold speeds up diffusion; cold slows molecular motion.
Diffusion is sometimes thought to stop at low temperatures; it slows but does not stop.

glossary_entry.definition: The speed at which molecules spread from high to low concentration.

flashcard.front: Does diffusion happen faster in hot water or cold water?
flashcard.back: Faster in hot water because molecules have more kinetic energy and move more rapidly.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Higher temperature increases kinetic energy, increasing diffusion rate. Cold slows molecules (A), diffusion is temperature-dependent (C false), and it does not stop (D false).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What happens to a red blood cell when it is placed in a hypertonic solution?

options.A: It swells and bursts due to water gain
options.B: It shrinks due to water loss
options.C: It remains unchanged because the solutions are isotonic
options.D: It becomes turgid but does not burst

questions.hint: Hypertonic means higher solute concentration outside the cell.
questions.explanation: In a hypertonic solution, water moves out of the red blood cell by osmosis, causing the cell to shrink (crenate). Option A describes hypotonic, C isotonic, and D describes plant cells, not animal cells.

source_trace.content_anchor: If the surrounding solution has a low concentration of water (hypertonic) than the cells, water will pass out of the cell by osmosis and the cell will shrink.

explanations_tiered.definition: A hypertonic solution has higher solute concentration outside, causing water to exit the cell and the cell to shrink.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Red blood cells in hypertonic solutions lose water by osmosis. The cell shrinks and becomes crenated. This can be reversed by placing the cell back in an isotonic solution.
explanations_tiered.beginner: In salty water (hypertonic), a red blood cell loses water and shrinks like a grape turning into a raisin.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Water moves down its concentration gradient from inside the cell (more water) to the hypertonic solution outside (less water). The red blood cell shrinks, a process called crenation.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Crenation occurs due to exosmosis. The cell volume decreases, increasing intracellular osmolarity. Severe crenation can impair cell function and deformability, affecting oxygen transport.
reasoning_steps: Recall that hypertonic means more solute outside.
Recall that water moves out of the cell in hypertonic conditions.
Identify that water loss causes the cell to shrink.
misconceptions: Some think hypertonic causes bursting; hypotonic causes bursting in animal cells.
Crenation is sometimes confused with turgor; turgor is plant-specific and positive.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Hypertonic → water out → animal cell shrinks.

glossary_entry.definition: The shrinking of an animal cell when placed in a hypertonic solution due to water loss.

flashcard.front: What happens to a red blood cell in salt water?
flashcard.back: Water leaves the cell by osmosis, causing the red blood cell to shrink (crenate).

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Water leaves the red blood cell in hypertonic solution, causing shrinkage. Swelling/bursting (A) is hypotonic, no change (C) is isotonic, turgor (D) is for plant cells.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is an organ?

options.A: A group of similar cells performing a common function
options.B: A structure composed of multiple tissues working together
options.D: A group of organs that work together to perform a major function

questions.hint: Think about the levels above tissue and below organ system.
questions.explanation: An organ is a structure made of two or more different tissues that work together to perform a specific function (e.g., the heart). Option A defines a tissue, C defines a cell, and D defines an organ system.

source_trace.content_anchor: Organs body structures composed of several different tissues that act as structural and functional units.

explanations_tiered.definition: An organ is a functional unit composed of two or more tissue types working together.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Organs perform complex functions that require cooperation between different tissues. For example, the stomach contains epithelial, muscle, and connective tissues to digest food.
explanations_tiered.beginner: An organ is like a toolbox that contains different tools (tissues) working together to build something.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Organs are at the third level of organization. The heart contains cardiac muscle tissue, nervous tissue, and connective tissue all working together to pump blood.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Organs arise from organogenesis during development. They show emergent properties: the function of the whole organ cannot be predicted solely from the properties of its individual tissues (e.g., the heart's rhythmic contraction).
reasoning_steps: Recall that organs are above tissues in hierarchy.
Recall that organs contain multiple tissue types.
Select the option describing multiple tissues working together.
misconceptions: Some think organs are made of cells directly; they are made of tissues first.
The heart is sometimes thought to be a tissue; it is an organ composed of multiple tissues.

glossary_entry.definition: A structure composed of multiple tissues that work together to perform a specific function.

flashcard.back: An organ is a structure made of multiple tissues working together, such as the heart or liver.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. An organ consists of multiple tissues. A defines tissue, C defines cell, D defines organ system.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A plant cell is placed in a solution and the central vacuole decreases in size, causing the plant to wilt. What type of solution was the cell placed in?
questions.hint: Wilting means the plant lost water.
questions.explanation: In a hypertonic solution, water leaves the plant cell, the vacuole shrinks, turgor pressure decreases, and the plant wilts. Hypotonic would make it turgid, isotonic no change, saturated is not a standard osmotic term.

source_trace.content_anchor: If a plant cell is surrounded by a solution less dilute than its contents (hypertonic solution), plant cells lose water and the cell gets plasmolysed.

explanations_tiered.definition: A hypertonic solution causes water to leave plant cells, reducing turgor pressure and causing wilting.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Water loss from the central vacuole reduces turgor pressure, making the plant cell flaccid. If severe, plasmolysis occurs and the plant wilts.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A plant in salty water (hypertonic) loses water, its vacuole shrinks, and it droops like a deflated balloon.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In a hypertonic solution, water exits the vacuole by osmosis. The vacuole shrinks, turgor pressure drops, and the plant becomes flaccid (wilts).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The decrease in vacuolar volume reduces turgor pressure \(P\) to zero, causing the plasma membrane to detach from the cell wall (plasmolysis). This is reversible if the cell is returned to a hypotonic solution.
reasoning_steps: Observe that the vacuole shrank (water left).
Recall that water leaves cells in hypertonic solutions.
Match water loss to hypertonic condition.
misconceptions: Some think wilting is caused by too much water; it's caused by too little water (hypertonic external).
Hypertonic is sometimes thought to make plants turgid; the opposite is true.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Hypertonic → water out → vacuole shrinks → wilting.

glossary_entry.definition: The drooping of plant parts due to loss of turgor pressure from water deficit.

flashcard.front: Why does a plant wilt in salty soil?
flashcard.back: The soil is hypertonic, water leaves the plant cells, reducing turgor pressure.

explanations_tiered.workbook_section: 3.3 Cell structure and function / 3.7 Cell environment
explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The vacuole shrank because water left the cell in a hypertonic solution. Hypotonic would swell (A), isotonic no change (C), saturated not an osmotic term (D).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The sodium-potassium pump moves sodium ions out of a cell and potassium ions into the cell, both against their concentration gradients. What type of transport is this, and what does it require?

options.A: Passive transport, requiring a concentration gradient

questions.hint: Moving against a gradient requires energy.
questions.explanation: Moving ions against their concentration gradients requires energy, so this is active transport, specifically primary active transport using ATP. Options A, C, and D describe passive processes that do not move substances against gradients.

source_trace.content_anchor: This particular case of active transport (called the sodium-potassium pump) is vital to the nervous system of most animals.

explanations_tiered.definition: The sodium-potassium pump is an active transport protein that uses ATP to move sodium and potassium against their gradients.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The pump maintains resting membrane potential and osmotic balance. It moves 3 Na⁺ out and 2 K⁺ in per ATP hydrolyzed, both against steep concentration gradients.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The sodium-potassium pump is like a bouncer that uses energy to push sodium out and pull potassium in, even when they don't want to go.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: This is primary active transport because it directly uses ATP. The pump changes shape when ATP is hydrolyzed, moving ions against their electrochemical gradients.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase is a P-type pump that phosphorylates itself during the cycle. It generates an electrochemical gradient used for secondary active transport (e.g., glucose uptake) and is responsible for nerve impulse propagation.
reasoning_steps: Identify that moving against a gradient requires energy.
Recall that active transport uses ATP.
Match the description to active transport.
misconceptions: Some think the sodium-potassium pump is passive; it is actively pumping against gradients.
The pump is sometimes thought to move sodium into the cell; it moves sodium out.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Na⁺/K⁺ pump: active transport, uses ATP.

glossary_entry.definition: An active transport protein that moves sodium out of and potassium into cells against their gradients using ATP.

flashcard.front: What type of transport is the sodium-potassium pump?
flashcard.back: It is active transport because it moves ions against their concentration gradients using ATP.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Moving against gradients requires energy (ATP), so this is active transport. Passive transport (A), osmosis (C), and diffusion (D) do not use ATP or move against gradients.
